title: Fix diagnostics error-code count after unreachable-code addition
description: The compiler-tests job is failing because sigil-diagnostics::codes::tests::count_error_codes still expects 124 codes after SIGIL-TYPE-UNREACHABLE-CODE was added for ai-pl-wlj. Update the count expectation and rerun the relevant diagnostics/compiler tests.

title: Design canonical typed CLI parsing above §process.argv()
description:
Problem
Sigil already has a canonical way to obtain argv: §process.argv(). That means raw argument access is NOT the missing feature. The missing feature is a Sigil-owned canonical way to parse raw argv into typed command selection, flags, options, operands, and help/error reporting without every project hand-rolling its own parser logic.

Why this issue exists
This issue is justified only if it establishes one blessed parsing surface on top of the existing raw argv surface. It must not create “another way to get args.” The current repo already contains manual argv parsing logic in first-party Sigil code, which is a smell if Sigil wants one canonical way of doing common tooling work.

Concrete evidence in the repo
- projects/repoAudit/src/main.sigil calls •repoAudit.runFromArgs(§process.argv())
- projects/repoAudit/src/repoAudit.lib.sigil contains hand-written argument parsing logic for check selection
- projects/topology-tcp/src/tcpRoundtripServer.sigil also reads §process.argv()

Scope to investigate
- a small stdlib surface such as §cli or §args that turns [String] into a typed exact record / sum / Result
- one canonical way to describe subcommands, flags, options, and positional args
- one canonical error/help model for parse failures and usage rendering
- interaction with existing Sigil run/test/debug CLI forwarding of argv
- first-party migration target(s), especially repoAudit, to prove the surface is better than hand-written parsing and should replace it

Philosophy safeguards
- Raw argv retrieval already exists. Do NOT add a second raw argv access surface.
- Do NOT add a parser-combinator library, a builder DSL, and a manual helper layer all at once. Pick one canonical approach.
- Do NOT add shell-specific convenience forms that fight Sigil’s typed/canonical goals.
- Do NOT add several equivalent ways to express subcommands or flags.
- If the new surface cannot clearly replace hand-written first-party parsing in repoAudit and similar tooling, it is probably too weak or too abstract.

Important design questions for the implementing session
1. What is the one canonical representation of a CLI specification in Sigil: exact records and sums, declarative descriptors, or a smaller helper pattern?
2. How should parse failure be represented: Result with a structured error and a canonical render-to-help function, or something else?
3. Should help/usage text be auto-derived from the canonical command description, and if so, how much formatting belongs in stdlib?
4. What is the smallest feature set that still replaces repo-local manual parsing: booleans, valued flags, positionals, subcommands, repeated args?
5. How does this stay obviously superior to project-local hand-rolled parsing while still remaining small and canonical?

Suggested repo entry points
- language/stdlib/process.lib.sigil
- language/docs/STDLIB.md
- language/spec/stdlib-spec.md
- projects/repoAudit/src/main.sigil
- projects/repoAudit/src/repoAudit.lib.sigil
- projects/topology-tcp/src/tcpRoundtripServer.sigil
- language/README.md (tooling positioning)

Definition of success
When this issue is done, Sigil still has exactly one way to obtain raw argv (§process.argv()), and now also exactly one Sigil-owned way to parse those args into typed command structure. First-party tooling examples should migrate to that surface so future projects have an obvious canonical pattern to copy.
design:
Design intent
This feature should sit above §process.argv(), not beside it. Treat §process.argv() as the canonical raw input surface and the new module as the canonical typed parsing surface.

Canonicality rule
The work is only aligned with Sigil philosophy if it reduces the number of reasonable first-party parsing patterns. If it leaves repoAudit-style custom parsing as equally legitimate next to a new stdlib parser, then the feature failed philosophically even if the code works.

Likely constraints
- one specification shape
- one parse result shape
- one canonical help/error rendering path
- strong fit with exact records, sums, and Result-based failure
- docs/examples should explicitly say that hand-written argv parsers are no longer the preferred first-party pattern when the canonical parser applies

Anti-goals
- no second argv access API
- no argparse clone with many equivalent declaration forms
- no reflection-heavy magic that obscures the exact typed result
acceptance_criteria:
1. The issue resolves with a design that preserves §process.argv() as the canonical raw argv source and adds, at most, one canonical typed parsing surface above it.
2. The design clearly says what existing first-party hand-written parsing should migrate, especially repoAudit.
3. The parse model, failure/help model, and supported command/flag/operand subset are all specified in enough detail to implement without reopening basic philosophy debates.
4. The issue explicitly rejects multiple equivalent CLI declaration styles.
5. Docs/spec/examples/tests are included in the completion plan.
notes: Ranked feature candidate #2. Important: raw argv access already exists; this task is about canonical parsing above that baseline, not about duplicating §process.argv().

title: Design canonical topology-backed database and queue surfaces
description:
Problem
Sigil already has a strong canonical story for named runtime boundaries: HTTP, TCP, FS, log, process, PTY, WebSocket, topology handles, env-selected worlds, and typed stdlib surfaces. It does NOT yet have the equivalent Sigil-owned story for databases and queues. In real projects this pushes users toward ad hoc typed FFI, project-local bridge modules, and one-off conventions for connection handles, query execution, enqueue/dequeue semantics, and test fixtures.

Why this issue exists
The motivation is not “make DB access shorter.” The motivation is to add a genuinely new canonical capability that Sigil does not currently own. Today the only general answer is “use FFI,” but FFI is the escape hatch, not the language-owned operational surface. If Sigil wants a real backend story, it needs one blessed database/queue boundary model that fits topology/config/world/test semantics the same way HTTP/TCP already do.

Scope to investigate
- topology handle forms for database and queue boundaries declared in src/topology.lib.sigil
- config/<env>.lib.sigil binding story for those handles inside the selected †runtime.World
- stdlib modules such as §sqlite / §postgres / §queue, or a narrower first slice if that is the better canonical starting point
- exact value/result/error shapes for query/request/response operations
- testing and replay fixtures so database/queue behavior is deterministic in tests and inspectable through world fixtures
- docs/spec/examples/tests updates needed to make the new surface the canonical first-party answer

Philosophy safeguards
- Do NOT treat this as permission to add multiple equivalent database surfaces. If Sigil chooses a database boundary story, that story becomes the one first-party way.
- Do NOT ship “raw FFI is still the normal answer, but here is also §postgres.” The whole point is to replace ad hoc first-party patterns with one Sigil-owned model.
- Do NOT add an ORM, query-builder zoo, or several competing transaction APIs. Prefer a small typed capability surface over framework ambition.
- Do NOT bypass topology. The design should preserve the existing rule that application code uses named handles, while config/world code binds those handles to concrete runtime implementations.
- Do NOT invent special cases that undermine the existing topology split. Topology declares named boundaries; config binds runtime truth.
- If a generic “database” abstraction makes the API weaker or blurrier than concrete modules, prefer concrete modules with one canonical shape each.

Important design questions for the implementing session
1. Is the right first slice “SQLite + queue” or “Postgres + queue” or “just queue” or “just one database surface first”? The answer should optimize for canonical ownership, testability, and immediate usefulness rather than maximum breadth.
2. Should queues be modeled as topology-owned named dependencies with separate producer/consumer handles, or as one handle with distinct stdlib operations?
3. What are the minimal exact types needed for successful operations, failure channels, and topology/config binding?
4. How should transactions, if any, fit the Owned[T] / using model so resource lifetime remains explicit and affine where appropriate?
5. What test-world and observe/check surfaces are needed so the new feature is not only runtime-real but also testable in canonical Sigil style?

Suggested repo entry points
- language/docs/topology.md
- language/docs/STDLIB.md
- language/docs/FFI.md
- language/spec/stdlib-spec.md
- language/stdlib/httpClient.lib.sigil
- language/stdlib/tcpClient.lib.sigil
- language/stdlib/process.lib.sigil
- language/world/
- projects/topology-http/
- projects/topology-tcp/
- projects/labelled-boundaries/

Definition of success
When this issue is done, another Sigil project should be able to declare named DB/queue boundaries, bind them in config, call them through a small blessed stdlib surface, test them with deterministic worlds, and read docs/examples that make it obvious this is the canonical Sigil way rather than an optional side path.
design:
Design intent
This feature should extend Sigil’s existing architecture rather than invent a side architecture. The likely center of gravity is:
- topology-owned named handles
- env-selected config/world binding
- small stdlib operational modules
- typed result/error values
- deterministic test fixtures

Canonicality rule
The implementing session must actively look for places where a new DB/queue API would merely duplicate an existing first-party capability. If a proposed operation is already adequately covered by an existing canonical Sigil surface, do not add another spelling. The issue is justified only where there is currently no Sigil-owned operational surface and users would otherwise fall back to ad hoc FFI.

Likely constraints
- application code should call named handles, not raw DSNs/URLs/connection strings
- config should own the environment-specific binding of those handles
- error channels should be typed and explicit, similar to current HTTP/TCP/Process practice
- any resourceful session/transaction/listener handle should respect Owned[T] and using if lifetime matters
- docs/spec/examples/tests must land together; this is not a parser-only or stdlib-only change

Anti-goals
- no ORMs
- no multiple “levels” of API that are equally canonical
- no hidden global connection resolution
- no silent runtime magic that bypasses inspect/test/world tooling
acceptance_criteria:
1. The issue resolves with one documented canonical plan for database and queue boundaries that is explicitly topology-aware rather than FFI-first.
2. The plan names the proposed canonical module ownership and handle/binding story, including how src/topology.lib.sigil, config/<env>.lib.sigil, and application code each participate.
3. The design explicitly states anti-goals that prevent multiple equivalent DB/queue APIs from shipping.
4. The implementation path includes docs/spec/examples/tests, not only runtime or stdlib code.
5. At least one concrete first-party example project path is identified as the proving ground for the feature.
notes: Ranked feature candidate #1 from the philosophy-constrained brainstorming pass. This task should be read together with the existing topology/config/world model, not as a greenfield feature request.

title: Implement first host-app tranche: subscriptions, owned resources, task/timer/http/process completion
description: Implement the first host-app tranche for Sigil so real local/server apps can move more orchestration out of TypeScript. This includes narrow foreign subscription interop for typed externs, an owned resource plus using-scope lifetime model, and Node-first app-runtime completion in stdlib: stream fanout, task/timer primitives, process ergonomics, and an app-grade HTTP request-stream surface. Browser/view work and sqlite are explicitly out of scope.
design: Keep the surface small and canonical. Foreign callback/listener APIs should enter Sigil only through a narrow typed-extern subscription form that produces owned stream sources. Add a compiler-known Owned[T] resource model with using scopes and non-escaping borrowed handles. Extend stdlib/runtime to support fanout hubs, task spawning, timer event sources, runChecked/runJson, and request-stream HTTP servers. Update parser, validator, typechecker, codegen, runtime helpers, examples, docs, and specs together.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Typed externs support subscribes members that elaborate to owned stream sources. 2. Owned[T] and using are parsed, typechecked, codegenerated, and enforced as non-duplicable scoped resources. 3. Stream fanout hubs, task spawning, timer event sources, process runChecked/runJson, and request-stream HTTP server APIs exist with tests. 4. PTY/WebSocket/FsWatch creators and other runtime-owned handles use the new ownership model consistently. 5. Examples, docs, and specs are updated to present the new canonical surfaces. 6. The compiler/runtime test suite passes for touched areas.

title: Lazy-load PTY and WebSocket runtime bridges for fixture-only tests
description: CI runs fixture example tests for PTY and WebSocket without installing language/runtime/node dependencies. The generated runtime currently imports pty-runtime.mjs and websocket-runtime.mjs eagerly at module startup, which fails even when the active world only uses fixtures. Change codegen to load those helpers lazily when a real PTY or WebSocket world entry is actually used, then verify fixture tests pass without language/runtime/node/node_modules present.

title: Fix rustfmt drift after websocket surface changes
description: CI is failing on cargo fmt --all --check after the websocket/runtime-helper changes. Apply the pinned rustfmt toolchain formatting to the touched Rust files and verify the compiler workspace is clean under the same command CI runs.

title: Add §pty as the canonical interactive session surface
description: Introduce a stdlib PTY surface for interactive coding sessions such as Claude Code and Codex. This should give Sigil one typed, world-aware abstraction for spawning a PTY-backed session, writing input, consuming output events, resizing, and waiting for exit, instead of forcing projects to speak directly to node-pty through ad hoc FFI.
design: Add core stdlib module §pty with a small Session/Event model and topology-aware spawn support. V1 should cover spawn/spawnAt, write, resize, events, close, and wait. Use §stream as the output substrate. World/testing should capture PTY spawns, writes, resizes, closes, and scripted output/exit traces. Backend can use node-pty on macOS/Node, but the package choice remains an implementation detail behind the Sigil surface.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Sigil defines canonical §pty types for Spawn, Session, and Event. 2. V1 supports spawn, write, resize, close, wait, and streaming output/exit events. 3. Named-boundary topology support exists for PTY launch policy. 4. Test worlds can script PTY behavior and inspect PTY traces with ※observe / ※check helpers. 5. The public API stays small and does not expose node-pty option sprawl.

title: Add §stream as the canonical long-lived event source
description: Introduce a minimal stdlib stream surface for long-lived runtime events in Sigil. V1 should stay intentionally tiny and pull-based so host callbacks can be normalized into a canonical Sigil abstraction without importing Rx-style operator sprawl. This is the substrate needed by PTY, WebSocket, and filesystem watch surfaces.
design: Add core stdlib module §stream with a minimal pull API: Source[T], Next[T]=Item(T)|Done(), next(source), and close(source). Model it as a runtime capability suitable for Node-backed implementations, but keep producer-specific observation in test worlds rather than adding generic stream traces. Keep the surface host-agnostic, callback-free, and narrow enough to remain canonical.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Sigil defines a canonical §stream surface with Source[T], Next[T], next, and close. 2. The runtime/codegen can host long-lived event sources behind that API on macOS/Node. 3. The design explicitly avoids combinator sprawl, push callbacks, and package-specific semantics. 4. Docs/specs describe §stream as the standard substrate for PTY/WebSocket/fsWatch-style sources.

title: Fix Homebrew formula install paths
description: Homebrew install of inerte/tap/sigil fails with `TypeError: Pathname.new requires a String, #to_path or #to_str`. The generated formula uses glob-based `Dir["*/sigil"].first` and `Dir["*/README.txt"].first` during install. Homebrew stages the tarball at the extracted root, so those globs can resolve to nil and break installation. Update the formula generator and checked-in formula to install directly from the extracted root.
design: Replace the glob-based install lines with direct root-relative installs for the staged release contents, e.g. `bin.install "sigil"` and `pkgshare.install "README.txt"`. Keep the release tarball layout unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The generated Homebrew formula installs Sigil successfully from the release tarball. 2. The install method no longer relies on globbing a nested extraction directory. 3. The checked-in packaging/homebrew formula matches the generator output. 4. A local Homebrew install path is verified on macOS.

title: Add embedded sigil docs retrieval commands
description: Installed Sigil binaries need an LLM-friendly way to discover the language without browsing the repo or website. Add a top-level `sigil docs` command family that searches and shows an embedded local corpus built from the repo docs/specs/articles.
design: Embed the docs corpus directly into the `sigil` binary at build time. Add `sigil docs list`, `search`, `show`, and `context` subcommands. Keep `sigil help` as plain text, but list `docs` so LLMs can discover it. `sigil docs` commands return JSON by default.
acceptance_criteria: `sigil help` lists `docs`; `sigil docs list/search/show/context --list/context <id>` work against an embedded corpus built from guides, docs, specs, grammar, and articles; outputs are machine-readable JSON; tests cover corpus ids, search windows, ranges, context bundles, and error cases.

title: Add compiler-owned local compile cache
description: Add a compiler-owned local cache for successful compile results so repeated sigil compile, run, test, and validate invocations can skip analysis/codegen when the effective inputs have not changed. Keep repoAudit-specific reporting and changed-roots policy out of this first cache layer; those stay in ai-pl-2lt.

title: Speed up repoAudit repo-compile by batching explicit roots
description: repoAudit repo-compile is currently dominated by a repo-root 'sigil compile .' sweep plus a second config-project pass. Replace that orchestration with explicit tracked-root compile targets and bounded concurrent execution so repoAudit keeps full coverage without the expensive repo-wide walk.
design: Derive canonical compile targets from tracked Sigil roots, keep dependency installs serial, run compile targets through a named concurrent region with deterministic merge order, and preserve the existing repoAudit issue envelope.
acceptance_criteria: 1. repo-compile no longer shells out to a repo-root 'sigil compile .'. 2. repoAudit compiles language plus tracked project roots explicitly, using '--env test' only for config-aware projects. 3. Full 'pnpm sigil:test:repo-audit' completes with the same JSON envelope and materially faster repo-compile behavior.

title: Remove pnpm requirement from Sigil runtime and test execution
description: `sigil run`, `sigil test`, and related runtime-backed commands currently shell out through `pnpm exec node --import tsx`, which couples end-user execution to a package manager and repo-local JS dependency layout. Track the work to run generated Sigil code with a leaner end-user runtime surface.
design: Prefer a runtime path that depends only on Node for execution. Keep package-manager usage limited to package publish/install workflows unless there is a hard technical requirement for runtime loading.
acceptance_criteria: End-user runtime-backed commands no longer require pnpm; `sigil run` and `sigil test` execute successfully with the supported Node runtime path alone; CLI/docs/install guidance are updated to reflect the required runtime dependency surface.

title: Add sigil init project scaffolding command
description: End users can install the Sigil CLI outside this monorepo, but there is no canonical command to scaffold a new project. Add a  command that creates the minimal canonical project layout and starter files for a standalone application or publishable package without requiring users to copy files from this repo.
design: Prefer one canonical neutral scaffold. `sigil init [path]` creates only the common project baseline: `sigil.json`, `src/`, `tests/`, and `.local/`. Do not choose app or package mode during init. Derive `name` from the target directory basename as lowerCamel and `version` from the current UTC timestamp. Hard-fail when the target is not safe to initialize.
acceptance_criteria: `sigil init` creates a canonical neutral Sigil project root with valid `sigil.json`, empty `src/`, empty `tests/`, and empty `.local/`; the command supports both `sigil init` in the current directory and `sigil init [path]`; docs and CLI tests cover generated layout, name/version derivation, and conflict errors.

title: Fix repoAudit runner hang on direct sigil run
description: While verifying ai-pl-h4e, 'sigil test projects/repoAudit/tests' passed but 'sigil run projects/repoAudit/src/main.sigil' hung locally with an idle Node/tsx process instead of exiting. Investigate open handles in the generated repoAudit runner and restore a clean repoAudit end-to-end verification path.
design: Inspect the generated repoAudit runtime path and any lingering process/file-system handles left open by the tsx/node execution flow. Keep the fix focused on runner cleanup rather than changing repoAudit check semantics.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Direct 'sigil run projects/repoAudit/src/main.sigil' exits cleanly after checks finish. 2. 'pnpm sigil:test:repo-audit' completes end-to-end without manual intervention. 3. The fix preserves current repoAudit check results.

title: Refactor feature flags to use rule-only rollout actions
description: The current feature-flag config model splits targeting across overrides, rules, and a top-level rollout. We want one canonical surface where ordered rules choose either a fixed value or a rollout action, so forced users, segmented rollouts, and plain percentage launches all use the same mechanism.
design: Keep config-level key selection, remove overrides and top-level rollout, introduce RuleAction=Value|Rollout, and make first matching rule win before declaration default. Update compiler/runtime validation, demos, docs/specs, and the existing feature-flag article together so the public model stays coherent.
acceptance_criteria: Feature flags use only key plus ordered rules with Value or Rollout actions; old overrides/top-level rollout configs are rejected; examples and package demos use the new shape; docs/specs/article/agents are updated; relevant tests pass.

title: Design canonical retry/backoff/timeout operational policy
description:
Problem
Sigil already has a way to express retries today: hand-written control flow using match, Result, and §time.sleepMs. That means this issue cannot be justified as mere shorthand. The gap is that operational retry/backoff/timeout policy is currently repo-local and ad hoc instead of Sigil-owned and canonical. Different projects can hand-roll slightly different loops, stop conditions, backoff curves, and timeout behavior.

Concrete evidence in the repo
- language/spec/stdlib-spec.md explicitly describes §time.sleepMs as the canonical delay primitive for retry loops and harness orchestration
- language/integrationTests/src/support.lib.sigil contains hand-written retry helpers built from §time.sleepMs and recursive control flow
- http/process surfaces already expose typed failure channels, including timeout-related errors in §httpClient and ProcessFailure in §process

Why this issue exists
The feature only makes sense if it defines one blessed operational policy surface that replaces repeated hand-written retry logic for the class of problems where Sigil wants a standard answer. It must not become “retry sugar in addition to the existing canonical way.” Match remains the branching surface. §time.sleepMs remains the primitive delay surface. The new work, if any, should own policy, not invent a second control-flow language.

Scope to investigate
- whether the canonical home is a small §retry module, or policy records attached to existing operational modules, or another narrow design
- one exact policy shape for attempts, delay/backoff, timeout budget, and retry predicate/classification
- how timeout interacts with existing typed error channels like HttpErrorKind.Timeout()
- deterministic test-world behavior for retry and timeout logic
- migration target(s) for current first-party hand-written retry helpers so the new surface proves it can replace them

Philosophy safeguards
- Do NOT add new general branching syntax. Match is already Sigil’s branching surface.
- Do NOT add sugar that simply shortens a loop people can already write. The bar is higher: one canonical operational policy surface that can replace ad hoc loops.
- Do NOT create inconsistent retry APIs on several modules. Prefer one policy shape or one owned module with a uniform model.
- Do NOT make retries implicit. Policy should stay explicit in source.
- Do NOT undermine deterministic testing. If the runtime behavior cannot be tested predictably in worlds/fixtures, the design is not finished.

Important design questions for the implementing session
1. Is the right center of gravity a dedicated §retry module, or should HTTP/process/task/client calls accept a shared retry policy record?
2. What is the single canonical backoff model: fixed, exponential, capped exponential, or one record that can express a chosen subset without creating many equivalent configurations?
3. How should total timeout budget be modeled relative to per-attempt failures and §time.sleepMs delays?
4. How are retryable failures classified across modules without creating leaky, module-specific one-offs?
5. Which current first-party hand-written helper should be migrated first to prove the design is real and worth owning?

Suggested repo entry points
- language/docs/ASYNC.md
- language/docs/STDLIB.md
- language/spec/stdlib-spec.md
- language/stdlib/time.lib.sigil
- language/stdlib/httpClient.lib.sigil
- language/stdlib/process.lib.sigil
- language/integrationTests/src/support.lib.sigil
- language/integrationTests/tests/httpClientServer.sigil
- language/integrationTests/tests/tcpClientServer.sigil

Definition of success
When this issue is done, Sigil still has match as the only branching surface and §time.sleepMs as the primitive delay surface, but it also has one canonical way to express operational retry/backoff/timeout policy so first-party code no longer hand-rolls slightly different loops for the same class of problem.
design:
Design intent
Keep the existing language philosophy intact:
- match remains the only public branching surface
- delay remains §time.sleepMs
- effects stay explicit
- concurrency policy remains an explicit source-level concept

Canonicality rule
The implementing session must be able to point to at least one existing hand-written retry helper and say “this new surface should replace that pattern in first-party Sigil code.” If it cannot replace a real existing pattern, it is probably just extra sugar and should not ship.

Likely constraints
- one policy shape
- typed failure handling
- explicit stop/retry classification
- deterministic world/test behavior
- documentation must explain when manual loops are still appropriate and when the canonical retry surface is required

Anti-goals
- no hidden automatic retries
- no new mini-language for branching
- no several retry builders with overlapping power
- no timeout semantics that ignore existing error/result conventions
acceptance_criteria:
1. The issue resolves with one canonical operational policy shape for retry/backoff/timeout or a clearly justified equivalent design that still preserves one-way philosophy.
2. The design preserves match as the only branching surface and §time.sleepMs as the primitive delay surface.
3. The issue explicitly identifies one or more current first-party hand-written retry helpers as migration targets.
4. Deterministic testing/world behavior is accounted for.
5. Docs/spec/examples/tests are included in the completion plan.
notes: Ranked feature candidate #4. Important: manual retry loops already exist; this task is only valid if it establishes a stronger canonical policy surface rather than parallel sugar.

title: Design canonical derived codecs/decoders for exact records and sums
description:
Problem
Sigil already has a canonical manual decode layer: §json for raw JSON values and §decode for turning them into trusted internal values. That existing split is good and should not be weakened. The problem is that exact records and nominal sums make many decoders mechanically repetitive, and first-party code is already paying that cost by hand in places like projects/todo-app/src/todoJson.lib.sigil and language/examples/trustedMessageDecode.sigil.

Why this issue exists
This issue is only justified if Sigil can define a strict boundary between cases that should use compiler- or stdlib-derived decoding and cases that should stay manual. If there is no clear boundary, derived codecs/decoders would just create a second equally valid style and violate the “one way” philosophy.

Current canonical baseline
- §json owns raw parse/inspect/stringify
- §decode owns decode/validate/trust promotion
- manual decoders are the current one way for all cases

Potential value
For plain shape-driven exact records and nominal sums, Sigil appears to have enough type information to derive a canonical decoder/codec surface. If that can be done without muddying refinements, labels, or custom boundary transforms, it would remove repetitive boilerplate while still preserving one blessed answer.

Philosophy safeguards
- Do NOT introduce a second equally canonical decoding style for the same plain-shape problem.
- If derived decoding ships, it should become the canonical first-party answer for the subset of cases it covers.
- Manual §decode must remain the canonical answer for genuinely custom decoding: cross-field logic, shape migrations, custom string/int transforms, refinement promotion that requires proof or domain logic, labelled-boundary policy interactions, or anything else outside the derivable subset.
- Do NOT add macro systems, decorator syntax, annotation zoos, or multiple derive mechanisms.
- Do NOT pretend labels or where-refinements are automatically runtime-validated unless the design explicitly models how that happens.

Important design questions for the implementing session
1. What exact subset is derivable? Exact records only? Nominal sums too? Option/list/map fields? Nested project-defined types?
2. What is the single canonical user-facing surface? A stdlib derivation helper, compiler-generated functions, or another mechanism that does not require call-site type arguments or generic lambdas that Sigil does not have?
3. How are sum encodings represented canonically in JSON? Tagged objects, single-field objects, or another one-way shape?
4. Where is the hard boundary where users must fall back to manual §decode?
5. Should derived encoding and derived decoding land together, or should decoding be the first canonical slice with encoding left manual for now?

Concrete evidence in the repo
- language/stdlib/decode.lib.sigil is the current manual decoder toolkit
- language/examples/trustedMessageDecode.sigil shows the current intended trust-promotion flow
- projects/todo-app/src/todoJson.lib.sigil contains repetitive record decode/encode plumbing that is a good test case

Suggested repo entry points
- language/stdlib/decode.lib.sigil
- language/docs/STDLIB.md
- language/spec/stdlib-spec.md
- language/docs/type-system.md
- language/examples/trustedMessageDecode.sigil
- projects/todo-app/src/todoJson.lib.sigil

Definition of success
When this issue is done, there is a crisp rule that tells a Sigil author which cases use the derived canonical codec/decoder surface and which cases still use manual §decode. The feature should reduce first-party boilerplate without producing two equally valid idioms for the same job.
design:
Design intent
Preserve the current boundary discipline:
raw input -> §json -> §decode / trusted internal values

Any derivation feature must slot into that discipline rather than replace it with a looser reflection story.

Canonicality rule
The implementing session should define the derivable subset first and refuse to expand beyond it unless each expansion still preserves one obvious answer. The worst outcome here would be “sometimes derive, sometimes manual, both are equally fine.” The right outcome is “for this exact subset, derived is the first-party rule; outside it, manual §decode is the rule.”

Likely constraints
- keep exact records exact
- keep nominal sums nominal
- be explicit about JSON representation of sums
- do not silently claim refinement/label validation unless implemented as part of the design
- if a compiler-assisted surface is chosen, sync docs/spec/examples/tests together

Anti-goals
- no macro/decorator ecosystem
- no parallel codec libraries
- no fuzzy partial record decoding model that undermines exact records
acceptance_criteria:
1. The issue resolves with a crisp derivable subset and an equally crisp fallback rule for manual §decode.
2. The design explicitly addresses exact records, nominal sums, and the treatment of refinements/labels/custom validation.
3. The chosen user-facing surface avoids introducing multiple derive mechanisms or unsupported generic-call syntax.
4. At least one current first-party manual decoder module is named as a migration/proof target.
5. Docs/spec/examples/tests are included in the completion plan.
notes: Ranked feature candidate #3. Important: the current manual §json + §decode pipeline is the baseline and should remain the fallback outside the derivable subset.

title: Update CI workflows for GitHub Actions Node 24
description: GitHub Actions warns that JavaScript actions still run on Node.js 20. Audit the workflows, update action versions or workflow env so CI runs without the Node 20 deprecation warning, and verify the affected workflows still make sense with the pinned toolchain/setup.

title: Prevent Rust formatting drift with CI and a pinned toolchain
description: Add a failing cargo fmt --check gate for the compiler workspace and pin the Rust toolchain so rustfmt behavior stays consistent across local development and CI.

title: Add §fsWatch as the canonical filesystem watch surface
description: Introduce a stdlib filesystem-watch surface for recursive directory watching on macOS/Node so Sigil applications can react to out-of-band worktree changes without polling. For syntarch this supports live UI refresh when users, git commands, or background tools modify a session worktree.
design: Add core stdlib module §fsWatch with a deliberately narrow advisory event model. V1 should expose watch(root), events(watch), and close(watch), with Event limited to Created(path), Changed(path), and Removed(path). Use §stream as the event substrate. Follow Node-style semantics first on macOS, with duplicate/coalesced events allowed and exact rename detection out of scope. Keep backend choice internal; implement the Sigil semantics directly instead of mirroring a watcher package surface.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Sigil defines a canonical §fsWatch surface with watch, events, and close. 2. Emitted paths are relative to the watched root. 3. V1 is usable on macOS/Node and explicitly documents advisory semantics, including possible duplicate or coalesced events. 4. §fsWatch integrates with test worlds through scripted watch events and observation helpers. 5. The public API avoids chokidar-style option sprawl, globs, ignore rules, and debounce controls.

title: Add §websocket as the canonical live UI push surface
description: Introduce a stdlib WebSocket surface so Sigil applications can push live updates to browser or UI clients without dropping to raw host code. For syntarch this is the canonical transport for session status, terminal output events, summaries, and activity updates.
design: Add core stdlib module §websocket with a server-first text-message API. V1 should cover listen, connections, messages, send, close, port, and wait. Use §stream for connection and message streams. Keep the surface to text messages only, with no client API, no binary frames, no subprotocol negotiation, and no broadcast helper in v1.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Sigil defines a canonical §websocket server surface. 2. V1 supports accepting connections, receiving text messages, sending text messages, closing clients, and waiting on the server. 3. Connection and message consumption use §stream. 4. Test worlds can script inbound connections/messages and observe outbound sends through ※observe / ※check helpers. 5. The API avoids host/package-specific option matrices.

title: Allow sigil init in non-empty directories
description: Relax `sigil init` so it can initialize an existing repository or working directory that already contains unrelated files such as `.git`, README files, or AGENTS.md. Initialization should still reject true conflicts like an existing `sigil.json` or pre-existing target paths that Sigil needs to create.
design: Change the init conflict check from blanket directory emptiness to targeted path conflict validation. Preserve safety by rejecting overwrites of Sigil-owned scaffold files/directories, but allow unrelated repository files to coexist.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `sigil init .` succeeds in a non-empty directory when no Sigil project metadata exists and required scaffold paths do not conflict. 2. Existing unrelated files and dot-directories do not block initialization. 3. Existing `sigil.json`, `src`, `tests`, or `.local` conflicts are still reported clearly when they would be overwritten or are incompatible. 4. CLI tests cover both the newly allowed case and the remaining conflict cases.

title: Reject dead pure expressions in wildcard sequencing
description: Add a compile error when `l _=(expr)` discards a pure expression in Sigil. Wildcard `let` is the canonical sequencing surface for effects, so discarding a pure result there should be rejected as dead code rather than silently allowed.
design: Implement this in typed canonical validation by checking `TypedExprKind::Let` with a wildcard pattern and `value.purity == Pure`. Reuse existing canonical validation style and diagnostics. Do not add an escape hatch or a pure-Unit exception in v1.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `l _=(expr)` fails compilation when `expr` is pure, including pure `Unit`. 2. Effectful wildcard sequencing remains allowed. 3. The rule lives in the existing typed canonical validation path and surfaces as a normal compile error. 4. Tests cover pure and effectful cases. 5. Docs/examples no longer show pure wildcard sequencing.

title: Reject unreachable code after terminating control flow
description: Add a compiler error for unreachable code in Sigil. This covers code that appears after a guaranteed-terminating control-flow boundary in the same execution path, such as statements after a return, fail/throw-style termination, or an exhaustively terminating branch. The goal is a hard compile error, not a warning, and the design should fit Sigil's explicit-effect and canonical-language goals.
design: Implement this as a control-flow reachability check in the compiler pipeline after parsing/validation has enough structure to reason about statement sequencing and terminating expressions. Start with syntactic/control-flow certainty only; do not attempt speculative whole-program proof. Keep the rule conservative: false negatives are acceptable for v1, false positives are not.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The compiler rejects unreachable code as an error, not a warning. 2. The checker catches straightforward cases after guaranteed termination in the same block/path. 3. Exhaustively terminating branches are handled conservatively and correctly. 4. Tests and docs/examples are updated to reflect the rule. 5. Error text makes it clear which code is unreachable and why.

title: Add repoAudit-local compile caching on top of compiler cache
description: Follow-up after compiler-owned compile caching lands. repoAudit should be able to add audit-specific orchestration such as per-check cache reporting, policy-specific reuse rules, or an explicit changed-roots fast path without coupling those decisions into the compiler's first cache layer. This issue is intentionally later work; the compiler-owned cache should land first and remain the source of truth for compile result reuse.

title: Add remote feature-flag providers beyond env snapshots
description: Feature flags currently support typed declarations plus static and deterministic env-backed snapshots. Track the next step for company-scale usage where values come from a shared service, support forced assignments, and propagate consistently across servers.
design: Keep the typed flag contract and deterministic evaluation model, but add a remote provider layer for shared live flag state, overrides, and refresh semantics.
acceptance_criteria: There is a tracked design and implementation path for service-backed feature-flag providers that preserves typed flags, deterministic bucketing, and cross-server consistency.

title: Let package install target a project path explicitly
description: The current package install flow is cwd-based. During the feature-flag demo this forced an extra branch in the workflow because the command could not target projects/featureFlagStorefront directly. Track path-aware package command ergonomics so automation and LLMs can install dependencies for a chosen project root without chdir assumptions.
design: Prefer one canonical invocation shape rather than keeping both cwd-only and path-specific flows ambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: Package install supports an explicit project path or there is one canonical path-aware package entrypoint with updated docs/tests.

title: Run cargo fmt --all as a deliberate workspace sweep
description: A full workspace rustfmt pass touches unrelated Rust files outside the current feature scope. Schedule a dedicated follow-up to run cargo fmt --all, review the formatter churn intentionally, and land it as its own focused change instead of mixing it into feature work.

title: Remove or justify unused ModuleGraph build_many path
description: The compiler test job still warns that ModuleGraph::build_many is dead code. Track either removing it or wiring it into a real call path so the core CLI crate stays warning-clean.
design: Prefer one explicit resolution: either delete the unused helper or add the missing caller and tests, rather than leaving dead code in a central module graph API.
acceptance_criteria: The compiler workspace no longer emits the dead-code warning for ModuleGraph::build_many, and the chosen path is covered by tests or direct call sites.
